title: Development notes
text: Open suest.Rproj, run:
code: install.packages(c(   "devtools",   "pkgdown",   "roxygen2",   "testthat" ))  devtools::document() devtools::test() devtools::check() pkgdown::build_site()

title: Development notes
text: Create new public repository named suest tdmize, package directory run: included GitHub Actions workflows : run R CMD check Windows, macOS, Linux; build deploy pkgdown site gh-pages branch; run full Mize, Doan, Long (2019) numerical acceptance suite. first successful pkgdown deployment, enable GitHub Pages using gh-pages branch GitHub done automatically.
code: git init git add . git commit -m "Initial suest package" git branch -M main git remote add origin https://github.com/tdmize/suest.git git push -u origin main

title: Example 6.1: Curvilinear effects and mediation
code: library(haven) library(marginaleffects) library(suest)  dat <- read_dta(   "https://tdmize.github.io/data/data/ah4_cme.dta" ) dat <- zap_labels(dat)  vars <- c(   "depsympB", "income", "age", "woman", "race", "jobsat" ) dat <- dat[complete.cases(dat[vars]), ] dat[c("woman", "race", "jobsat")] <-   lapply(dat[c("woman", "race", "jobsat")], factor)

what: Fit the base and mediator models
title: Example 6.1: Curvilinear effects and mediation
code: base <- lm(   depsympB ~ income + I(income^2) + age + woman + race,   data = dat )  mediator <- lm(   depsympB ~ income + I(income^2) + age + woman + race + jobsat,   data = dat )  combined <- suest(   base,   mediator,   model_names = c("Base", "Mediator") )

what: Compare a one-standard-deviation increase in income
title: Example 6.1: Curvilinear effects and mediation
text: expected effects approximately -0.816 -0.648 depressive-symptom units. difference approximately -0.168 robust standard error 0.022.
code: income_sd <- sd(dat$income)  forward_change <- function(amount) {   function(x) data.frame(lo = x, hi = x + amount) }  effects <- avg_comparisons(   combined,   variables = list(income = forward_change(income_sd)),   newdata = dat )  effects hypotheses(effects, hypothesis = difference ~ revpairwise)

title: Example 6.2: Nested logit models
code: library(haven) library(marginaleffects) library(suest)  gss <- zap_labels(read_dta(   "https://tdmize.github.io/data/data/gss_cme.dta" ))  vars <- c(   "vhappy", "college", "age", "married", "parent", "woman",   "conserv", "reltrad", "year", "employed" )  dat <- subset(gss, year >= 2000 & employed == 1) dat <- dat[complete.cases(dat[vars]), ] dat[c("college", "married", "parent", "woman", "conserv",       "reltrad", "year")] <-   lapply(dat[c("college", "married", "parent", "woman", "conserv",                "reltrad", "year")], factor)

title: Example 6.2: Nested logit models
code: model1 <- glm(   vhappy ~ college,   family = binomial("logit"),   data = dat )  model2 <- glm(   vhappy ~ college + married + parent + woman + conserv +     reltrad + year + age + I(age^2),   family = binomial("logit"),   data = dat )  combined <- suest(   model1,   model2,   model_names = c("Model 1", "Model 2") )

what: Compare the average effect of college
title: Example 6.2: Nested logit models
text: expected average effects 0.0718 0.0599. cross-model difference 0.0119 robust standard error 0.0040.
code: effects <- avg_comparisons(   combined,   variables = "college",   newdata = dat )  effects hypotheses(effects, hypothesis = difference ~ revpairwise)

title: Example 6.3: Alternative predictor operationalizations
code: library(haven) library(marginaleffects) library(suest)  gss <- zap_labels(read_dta(   "https://tdmize.github.io/data/data/gss_cme.dta" ))  vars <- c(   "samesexB", "sexident", "sexbehav", "college",   "woman", "race", "age", "year" ) dat <- gss[complete.cases(gss[vars]), ] dat[c("sexident", "sexbehav", "college", "woman", "race", "year")] <-   lapply(dat[c("sexident", "sexbehav", "college", "woman",                "race", "year")], factor)

what: Fit models using behavior and identity
title: Example 6.3: Alternative predictor operationalizations
code: behavior <- glm(   samesexB ~ sexbehav + woman + college + age + race + year,   family = binomial("logit"),   data = dat )  identity <- glm(   samesexB ~ sexident + woman + college + age + race + year,   family = binomial("logit"),   data = dat )  combined <- suest(   behavior,   identity,   model_names = c("Behavior", "Identity") )

title: Example 6.3: Alternative predictor operationalizations
text: predictor appears one component model, marginaleffects reports zero NA structurally absent effects. nonzero rows give effects operationalization.
code: effects <- avg_comparisons(   combined,   variables = list(     sexbehav = "reference",     sexident = "reference"   ),   newdata = dat )  effects

title: Example 6.3: Alternative predictor operationalizations
text: expected cross-model contrasts approximately 0.177, 0.066, -0.111.
code: alternative_predictor_hypothesis <- function(x) {   group <- as.character(x$group)   term <- as.character(x$term)    behavior <- x$estimate[term == "sexbehav" & group == "Behavior"]   identity <- x$estimate[term == "sexident" & group == "Identity"]    data.frame(     term = c(       "Behavior bisexual - Identity bisexual",       "Behavior gay - Identity gay",       "(Behavior gay - bisexual) - (Identity gay - bisexual)"     ),     estimate = c(       behavior[1] - identity[1],       behavior[2] - identity[2],       (behavior[2] - behavior[1]) -         (identity[2] - identity[1])     )   ) }  avg_comparisons(   combined,   variables = list(     sexbehav = "reference",     sexident = "reference"   ),   newdata = dat,   hypothesis = alternative_predictor_hypothesis )

title: Example 6.4: Different count outcomes
code: library(haven) library(MASS) library(marginaleffects) library(suest)  gss <- zap_labels(read_dta(   "https://tdmize.github.io/data/data/gss_cme.dta" ))  vars <- c(   "mntlhlth", "physhlth", "woman", "married", "age",   "faminc", "race", "college", "parent", "year" ) dat <- gss[complete.cases(gss[vars]), ] dat[c("woman", "married", "parent", "college", "race", "year")] <-   lapply(dat[c("woman", "married", "parent", "college",                "race", "year")], factor)

what: Fit negative-binomial models for two outcomes
title: Example 6.4: Different count outcomes
text: suest() includes log(theta) negative-binomial model joint parameter vector, required reproduce robust uncertainty estimates.
code: mental <- glm.nb(   mntlhlth ~ woman + married + parent + college + age +     faminc + race + year,   data = dat )  physical <- glm.nb(   physhlth ~ woman + married + parent + college + age +     faminc + race + year,   data = dat )  combined <- suest(   mental,   physical,   model_names = c("Mental", "Physical") )

title: Example 6.4: Different count outcomes
text: example, one-standard-deviation age effects approximately -0.462 poor mental-health days 0.491 poor physical-health days. difference approximately -0.953 robust standard error 0.132.
code: forward_change <- function(amount) {   function(x) data.frame(lo = x, hi = x + amount) }  effects <- avg_comparisons(   combined,   variables = list(     woman = "reference",     married = "reference",     parent = "reference",     college = "reference",     age = forward_change(sd(dat$age)),     faminc = forward_change(sd(dat$faminc)),     race = "reference",     year = "reference"   ),   newdata = dat )  effects

title: Example 6.5: Ordered versus multinomial logit
code: library(haven) library(MASS) library(nnet) library(marginaleffects) library(suest)  gss <- zap_labels(read_dta(   "https://tdmize.github.io/data/data/gss_cme.dta" ))  vars <- c(   "partyid5", "woman", "edyrs", "age", "parent", "married",   "faminc", "employed", "region4", "year", "race" )  dat <- subset(gss, year >= 2010) dat <- dat[complete.cases(dat[vars]), ] dat[c("woman", "parent", "married", "race",       "employed", "region4", "year")] <-   lapply(dat[c("woman", "parent", "married", "race",                "employed", "region4", "year")], factor)  party_levels <- sort(unique(dat$partyid5)) dat$party_ord <- ordered(dat$partyid5, levels = party_levels) dat$party_nom <- factor(dat$partyid5, levels = party_levels)

title: Example 6.5: Ordered versus multinomial logit
code: ordered_model <- polr(   party_ord ~ age + I(age^2) + woman + edyrs + parent +     married + race + faminc + employed + region4 + year,   data = dat,   method = "logistic",   Hess = TRUE )  nominal_model <- multinom(   party_nom ~ age + I(age^2) + woman + edyrs + parent +     married + race + faminc + employed + region4 + year,   data = dat,   Hess = TRUE,   trace = FALSE )  combined <- suest(   ordered_model,   nominal_model,   model_names = c("Ordered", "Multinomial") )

title: Example 6.5: Ordered versus multinomial logit
text: standard empirical-distribution average, marginaleffects can calculate category-specific changes directly: published example evaluates change age 20 age 30 model-matrix columns held means. package’s full acceptance suite implements exact delta-method calculation reproduces Stata results:
code: avg_comparisons(   combined,   variables = list(age = function(x) {     data.frame(lo = x, hi = x + 10)   }),   newdata = dat )

title: Example 6.6: Different samples
code: library(haven) library(marginaleffects) library(suest)  gss <- zap_labels(read_dta(   "https://tdmize.github.io/data/data/gss_cme.dta" ))  vars <- c(   "helpsickB", "conserv", "faminc", "employed", "woman",   "age", "college", "married", "parent", "race", "year" ) dat <- gss[complete.cases(gss[vars]), ] dat[c("conserv", "employed", "woman", "college",       "married", "parent", "race")] <-   lapply(dat[c("conserv", "employed", "woman", "college",                "married", "parent", "race")], factor)

what: Fit the same model in two years
title: Example 6.6: Different samples
text: samples disjoint, printed object reports zero overlapping observations cross-model covariance block exactly zero.
code: model_1986 <- glm(   helpsickB ~ conserv + faminc + employed + woman + age +     college + married + parent + race,   family = binomial("logit"),   data = dat,   subset = year == 1986 )  model_2016 <- glm(   helpsickB ~ conserv + faminc + employed + woman + age +     college + married + parent + race,   family = binomial("logit"),   data = dat,   subset = year == 2016 )  combined <- suest(   model_1986,   model_2016,   model_names = c("1986", "2016") )  combined

what: Average within each model’s estimation sample
title: Example 6.6: Different samples
text: expected average effects approximately -0.092 1986 -0.258 2016. difference approximately 0.166 robust standard error 0.039.
code: nd <- suest_newdata(combined)  effects <- avg_comparisons(   combined,   variables = "conserv",   newdata = nd )  effects hypotheses(effects, hypothesis = difference ~ revpairwise)

title: Getting started with suest
code: library(suest) library(marginaleffects)

title: Getting started with suest
code: dat <- mtcars dat$am <- factor(dat$am)  base <- glm(am ~ wt, family = binomial(), data = dat) adjusted <- glm(am ~ wt + hp, family = binomial(), data = dat)

title: Getting started with suest
text: printed object reports component model types, comparison scale, component sample sizes, number overlapping observations, number parameters joint system.
code: combined <- suest(   base,   adjusted,   model_names = c("Base", "Adjusted") )  combined

title: Getting started with suest
text: final command directly tests difference effects. compare whether one effect statistically significant .
code: effects <- avg_comparisons(   combined,   variables = "wt",   newdata = dat )  effects  hypotheses(   effects,   hypothesis = difference ~ revpairwise )

title: Getting started with suest
text: models fitted different samples, use suest_newdata() average model’s effect estimation sample. disjoint samples, cross-model covariance zero, variance difference sum two component variances.
code: model_4cyl <- glm(   am ~ wt + hp,   family = binomial(),   data = dat,   subset = cyl == 4 )  model_other <- glm(   am ~ wt + hp,   family = binomial(),   data = dat,   subset = cyl != 4 )  combined_samples <- suest(   model_4cyl,   model_other,   model_names = c("Four cylinders", "Other") )  nd <- suest_newdata(combined_samples)  avg_comparisons(   combined_samples,   variables = "wt",   newdata = nd,   hypothesis = difference ~ revpairwise )

what: suest for comparing predictions and marginal effects across models
title: suest for comparing predictions and marginal effects across models
text: Full documentation replication examples: tdmize.github.io/suest suest combines two separately fitted regression models one object joint model-robust covariance matrix. combined object works marginaleffects compare predictions marginal effects across models. package implements framework developed : Mize, Trenton D., Long Doan, J. Scott Long. 2019. “General Framework Comparing Predictions Marginal Effects Across Models.” Sociological Methodology 49(1):152–189. doi:10.1177/0081175019852763

title: suest for comparing predictions and marginal effects across models
code: # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("tdmize/suest")

title: suest for comparing predictions and marginal effects across models
text: suest() accounts cross-model covariance estimates. essential models use overlapping observations.
code: library(suest) library(marginaleffects)  dat <- mtcars dat$am <- factor(dat$am)  base <- glm(am ~ wt, family = binomial(), data = dat) adjusted <- glm(am ~ wt + hp, family = binomial(), data = dat)  combined <- suest(   base,   adjusted,   model_names = c("Base", "Adjusted") )  effects <- avg_comparisons(   combined,   variables = "wt",   newdata = dat )  effects hypotheses(effects, hypothesis = difference ~ revpairwise)

title: suest for comparing predictions and marginal effects across models
text: linear regression using lm() binary logit probit using glm() Poisson regression using glm() negative-binomial regression using MASS::glm.nb() ordered logit probit using MASS::polr() multinomial logit using nnet::multinom() Models may use identical, partially overlapping, completely disjoint samples. supported cross-family comparisons logit–probit, logit–linear, probit–linear, Poisson–negative binomial, ordered logit–multinomial logit.

title: suest for comparing predictions and marginal effects across models
text: package website includes six R replications corresponding Examples 6.1–6.6 mecompare page: Curvilinear effects mediation Nested logit models Alternative predictor operationalizations Different count outcomes Ordered versus multinomial logit Different samples

title: Seemingly Unrelated Estimation for Model Comparisons — suest-package

what: Combine Two Fitted Models with Seemingly Unrelated Estimation — suest
title: Combine Two Fitted Models with Seemingly Unrelated Estimation — suest
text: Combines two separately fitted models constructs joint model-robust covariance matrix observation-level score contributions.

title: Combine Two Fitted Models with Seemingly Unrelated Estimation — suest
code: suest(model1, model2, model_names = NULL)  # S3 method for class 'suest_model' coef(object, ...)  # S3 method for class 'suest_model' vcov(object, ...)  # S3 method for class 'suest_model' nobs(object, ...)  # S3 method for class 'suest_model' print(x, ...)

title: Combine Two Fitted Models with Seemingly Unrelated Estimation — suest
text: model1, model2 Two supported fitted model objects. model_names Optional character vector containing two display names. object, x suest_model object. ... Additional arguments.

title: Combine Two Fitted Models with Seemingly Unrelated Estimation — suest
text: object class "suest_model" containing component models, joint coefficient vector, joint model-robust covariance matrix, sample-alignment information.

title: Combine Two Fitted Models with Seemingly Unrelated Estimation — suest
text: Exactly two models supported. Models may use identical, partially overlapping, disjoint samples. Supported model classes include linear, binary logit probit, Poisson, negative binomial, ordered logit probit, multinomial logit models. returned object works marginaleffects package.

title: Combine Two Fitted Models with Seemingly Unrelated Estimation — suest
code: dat <- mtcars dat$am <- factor(dat$am) model1 <- glm(am ~ wt, family = binomial(), data = dat) model2 <- glm(am ~ wt + hp, family = binomial(), data = dat) fit <- suest(model1, model2, model_names = c("Base", "Adjusted")) fit #> Seemingly Unrelated Estimation #> Models: Base + Adjusted  #> Model types: Base=logit, Adjusted=logit  #> Comparison scale: predicted probabilities  #> Observations: Base=32, Adjusted=32  #> Overlapping observations: 32  #> Union observations: 32  #> Parameters: 5

what: Stack the Estimation Samples from a SUEST Object — suest_newdata
title: Stack the Estimation Samples from a SUEST Object — suest_newdata
text: Creates model-specific new data using component model's estimation sample. useful effects averaged separately within different samples.

title: Stack the Estimation Samples from a SUEST Object — suest_newdata
code: suest_newdata(object)

title: Stack the Estimation Samples from a SUEST Object — suest_newdata
text: object suest_model returned suest().

title: Stack the Estimation Samples from a SUEST Object — suest_newdata
text: data frame containing component model frames internal model-routing columns.

title: suest 0.1.0
text: Initial GitHub release. Combines exactly two supported cross-sectional models using joint model-robust covariance matrix. Integrates marginaleffects predictions, comparisons, slopes, hypotheses, plots. Supports identical, partially overlapping, disjoint estimation samples. Includes replication vignettes Examples 6.1–6.6 Mize, Doan, Long (2019). Expanded numerical acceptance suite cover full cross-model comparison--model-family matrix, including nested/mediator comparisons, alternative predictor operationalizations, different outcomes, sex-stratified samples, missing-data sample changes, partially overlapping samples. Added -sample disjoint-sample tests every supported cross-family comparison. Imported stats generics used suest_model S3 methods namespace loads correctly R CMD check pkgdown builds. Updated GitHub Actions checkout steps actions/checkout@v6 GitHub Pages deployment action version 4.8.0. Corrected vignette metadata article unique VignetteIndexEntry. Updated exact-zero covariance tests ignore irrelevant matrix dimnames. Added local package pkgdown preflight script.
